nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, fast-flowing streams and smaller river systems with sandy or fine substrates, leaf litter, roots, and dense vegetation; found near river banks in the upper Araguaia basin in Goiás State

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential — protects delicate barbels)
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for refuge
dense fine-leaved plants (Java moss, Taxiphyllum, Cabomba)
shaded hiding spots and low-light areas

diet.notes: Foraging omnivore; in the wild feeds on worms, benthic crustaceans, insects, and plant matter. In the aquarium accepts high-quality sinking micro-pellets, bottom-feeder wafers, and frozen or live foods (bloodworm, daphnia, Tubifex, baby brine shrimp). A varied diet is important for conditioning; do not rely on this fish to survive on tank leftovers alone.

goodWith: small tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, green neon tetras)
small freshwater shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) in mature tanks
avoidWith: large or aggressive fish that may intimidate or outcompete them
boisterous hyper-active bottom feeders that outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species image; check license before use.

summary: The Sixray Corydoras is a miniature catfish (~3 cm) from the upper Araguaia basin in Brazil, distinguished from all other Aspidoras by having only 6 dorsal-fin rays (hence "pauciradiatus" = few-rayed). Though sold as a "False Corydoras," it belongs to the separate genus Aspidoras. It is cooler-tolerant than most cories and requires a group of 6+ for confident behaviour.
careNotes:
Aspidoras pauciradiatus is one of the smallest catfishes available in the hobby, reaching only about 2.5–3.5 cm. It is classified in the genus Aspidoras, which is distinct from Corydoras — the two genera differ in cranial morphology (Aspidoras lacks the nuchal plate found in Corydoras). Despite superficial similarity, this species should not be confused with Corydoras pygmaeus or C. habrosus; it is specifically distinguished from all other Aspidoras by possessing only 6 soft dorsal-fin rays rather than 7.

A mature, well-filtered aquarium of at least 54 L (60 × 30 cm footprint) is the minimum, though larger tanks are preferable when housing a proper group of 6 or more. Fine sand substrate is essential — rough gravel will damage the sensitive barbels, which the fish use to probe for food. Leaf litter (Indian almond leaves) and twisted roots replicate the natural biotope and help maintain the slightly acidic chemistry this species prefers.

Water quality must be excellent. Regular partial water changes are important; the species is sensitive to accumulated nitrates and stale water. Temperature tolerance is broader than many tropical catfishes — 20–27°C — making it suitable for cooler tropical or even subtropical setups. It will thrive alongside White Cloud Mountain minnows or Zebra danios that share the lower end of this range.

Like all callichthyid catfishes, A. pauciradiatus is a facultative air-breather and will periodically dash to the surface to gulp air — this is normal and the tank lid should be secure. The pectoral-fin locking spines are hardened and can pierce skin if handled carelessly; use a plastic container rather than a net when moving these fish. These spines carry a mild venom gland as is typical for callichthyid catfishes, so handle with care.

Keep in groups of at least 6 (8–10 strongly preferred) to see natural active foraging behaviour; specimens kept in small numbers tend to hide and are rarely seen.
breedingNotes: Breeding is considered difficult and requires significant preparation. Condition the group with varied live and frozen foods. Trigger spawning with a large partial water change (50–75%) using cooler, very soft, slightly acidic water (pH ~6.0, GH 0–1 dH) — mimicking a tropical rain event. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants or Java moss and are not guarded. Remove adults after spawning to prevent egg predation. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–5 days depending on temperature. Raise fry on infusoria, vinegar eels, and then micro-worms and newly hatched brine shrimp. A separate breeding tank is strongly recommended.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aspidoras pauciradiatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (30–35 mm), temperature (20–27°C), pH (6.0–7.0), hardness (36–215 ppm ≈ 2–12 dGH), minimum tank footprint (60 × 30 cm / ~54 L), native range (upper Araguaia basin, Goiás State, Brazil), breeding trigger (large cool water change), and diet (omnivore forager accepting sinking dried, live, and frozen foods).

additional.name: FishBase — Aspidoras pauciradiatus (species #12190)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), maximum size (2.9 cm SL), temperature (22–25°C), pH (6.0–7.2), native range (upper Araguaia basin, Brazil), benthopelagic habit, schooling behaviour, and facultative air-breathing. FishBase max size of 2.9 cm SL is standard length; total length used in profile (~3.5 cm) per SeriouslyFish and trade observations.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Aspidoras pauciradiatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temperature 22–25°C, pH 6.5–7.0, GH 8–12), adult size (3–3.5 cm), peaceful schooling behaviour, sandy substrate requirement, breeding notes (acid soft water, eggs among fine-leaved plants, fry on vinegar eels).

additional.name: Steenfott Aquatics — Sixray Cory Care Guide
additional.notes: Provided tank size guidance (minimum 38–75 L / 10–20 gal), compatibility recommendations (small tetras, rasboras, pencilfish, dwarf cichlids), substrate advice (fine sand essential), diet guidance (sinking pellets, frozen foods), and care level characterisation (moderate / intermediate).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Sixray corydoras
additional.notes: Cross-checked common names (sixray corydoras, false corydoras), taxonomy, native range (upper Araguaia River basin, Brazil), diet (worms, benthic crustaceans, insects, plant matter), egg-laying without parental care, and IUCN Least Concern status.

fish.breedingNotes: Requires very soft, acidic water (pH ~6.0, GH 0–1 dH) triggered by a large cool water change. Eggs scattered among fine-leaved plants; no parental care. Fry raised on infusoria, then vinegar eels and baby brine shrimp. A dedicated breeding tank is strongly recommended.
